Grab a pencil. Dog-ears are encouraged.

Human in the Loop: Misunderstood — Summer Edition

Part I — Foundations

  • What Is HITL ML?
  • A Taxonomy of Human–Machine Interaction

Part II — Core Techniques

  • Data Annotation and Labeling
  • Active Learning
  • Interactive Machine Learning

Part III — Learning from Human Feedback

  • Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback
  • Learning from Demonstrations
  • Learning from Comparisons and Rankings

Part IV — Applications

  • HITL in Natural Language Processing
  • HITL in Computer Vision
  • HITL in Healthcare and Science

Part V — Systems and Practice

  • Annotation Platforms and Tooling
  • Crowdsourcing and Quality Control
  • Evaluation and Metrics

Part VI — Ethics and Horizons

  • Fairness, Bias, and Ethics
  • Future Directions

Case Study

  • Limen: A Human in the Loop of Everything

Back

  • .md

Answers#

Word Search Answers#

(In the web version, found words highlight automatically. These are for the print edition.)

Chapter 1 — What Is HITL ML?#

  FEEDBACK       → row  4, col  6,  right
  THRESHOLD      → row  6, col  5,  right
  LOOP           → row  5, col 15,  left-down
  LIMEN          → row  8, col  5,  left-down
  CAUSAL         → row 10, col  7,  right-down
  ROUTING        → row  7, col  9,  right-down
  VOICE          → row 12, col 13,  left-up
  PRIVACY        → row 13, col 13,  left-up
  DESIGN         → row 15, col 14,  left-up

Expert Insights: Spot the Human#

These insights provide the technical “why” behind the interactive scenarios.

Chapter 4 — Active Learning (Hospital X-rays)#

The Core Issue: Uncertainty isn’t a monolith. In the scenario where the AI flags uncertain X-rays, the expert radiologist is dealing with Epistemic Uncertainty (the model hasn’t seen enough similar cases) vs. Aleatoric Uncertainty (the image itself is blurry or noisy). Takeaway: If the radiologist only sees “uncertain” cases, their own internal calibration of what “normal” looks like may drift. This is the Complacency Trap.

Chapter 11 — Healthcare (ECG Overruling)#

The Core Issue: Dataset Shift and Accountability. When the cardiologist overrules the AI, she is acting as the final safety layer. However, if the AI was trained on a different demographic (Dataset Shift), its “opinion” might be fundamentally biased for this specific patient. Takeaway: “Human-in-the-loop” here is a legal and ethical shield, but it only works if the human is actually paying more attention than the machine.

Chapter 14 — Evaluation (The 95% Accuracy Model)#

The Core Issue: The Base Rate Fallacy. If 95% of your cases are negative, a model that always says “negative” is 95% accurate but has zero utility. Takeaway: This is why we use Cohen’s Kappa. It adjusts accuracy by the amount of agreement you would get by just guessing (chance). For a model that predicts one class every time, Kappa would be 0.0, revealing the model’s true lack of predictive power.

The “Think about it” and “Spot the human” sections don’t have single right answers.

If you found yourself arguing with a question, that’s the right answer.

previous

Limen: A Human in the Loop of Everything

By Lazaros Toumanidis
